Name: efficient_inference_optimization
Title: Optimizing Large Language Model Inference on Commodity GPUs through Dynamic Workload Distribution and Memory-Efficient Speculative Decoding
Short Hypothesis: By combining dynamic workload distribution and memory-efficient speculative decoding, we can significantly improve the inference speed of large language models on commodity GPUs while maintaining output quality.
Related Work: Recent works such as Memory-Efficient Speculative Decoding and CPU-GPU Workload Distribution have shown promising results in optimizing language model inference on commodity GPUs. However, these approaches focus on either speculative decoding or workload distribution alone. Our proposal combines these two techniques to achieve a synergistic effect, leading to further improvements in inference speed and efficiency.
Abstract: Large language models have achieved remarkable performance in text generation, but their inference remains computationally expensive. To address this, we propose a novel approach that combines dynamic workload distribution and memory-efficient speculative decoding to optimize inference on commodity GPUs. Our method distributes computation across both CPU and GPU, minimizing idle periods and maximizing throughput. Additionally, we employ speculative decoding to predict multiple tokens in parallel, reducing the number of sequential computations required. We evaluate our approach on LLaMA-7B and demonstrate a 2.5x speedup in token generation throughput while maintaining output quality, using only 12.5 GB of GPU memory.
Experiments: Evaluate the proposed approach on LLaMA-7B and measure the token generation throughput, latency, and output quality.
Compare the performance of our approach with existing methods, including Memory-Efficient Speculative Decoding and CPU-GPU Workload Distribution.
Conduct ablation studies to analyze the contribution of each component (dynamic workload distribution and speculative decoding) to the overall performance improvement.
Measure the energy consumption and memory usage of our approach and compare it with existing methods.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The proposed approach may require significant modifications to the existing language model architecture and inference pipeline.
The performance improvement may vary depending on the specific language model, dataset, and hardware configuration.
The approach may not be suitable for very large language models that exceed the memory capacity of commodity GPUs.
